original_abstract: The mechanism by which copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions cleave carbon-halogen bonds has remained poorly understood, largely due to the diverse redox pathways available to copper and the high reactivity of the resulting high-valent intermediates. Here, we report the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitileres to both ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes, resulting in the formation of previously elusive alkyl copper(III) products. These complexes were fully characterized, and their unusual stability is attributed to the presence of a strong Cu–CF₃ bond combined with a substantial energy barrier that prevents C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination. Our mechanistic investigations, supported by kinetic studies and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal that oxidative addition follows two distinct trajectories depending on the nature of the precursor: the ionic copper(I) complex reacts primarily through an Sₙ2-type substitution, whereas the neutral complex proceeds via halogen-atom transfer (XAT). Notably, we observed significant ligand acceleration in the neutral system, a phenomenon that aligns with the reactivity patterns seen in the copper-catalyzed coupling of alkyl electrophiles with amines, azoles, and alkynes. These findings provide a rare, detailed look at the fundamental steps of copper-mediated bond formation.
humanized_abstract: The mechanism of the homolytic cleavage of carbon–halogen bonds in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions remains unclear due to the complexity of copper redox processes and the instability of the highly reactive cuprate species involved. Here, we show that α-haloacetonitriles react with both ionic and neutral copper(I) species in an oxidative addition manner to give the first examples of alkyl copper(III) species. These cuprate species were fully characterised and were shown to be remarkably stable due to a strong Cu–CF₃ bond and a high activation energy barrier for the C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination process. A detailed mechanistic study based on kinetic and density functional theory (DFT) calculations showed that oxidative addition occurs by two different routes, depending on the copper(I) species used: the ionic species undergoes an Sₙ2 reaction analogue and the neutral species reacts via a halogen-atom transfer (XAT) mechanism. Ligand acceleration of the neutral copper(I) species was observed, a phenomenon that is also present in the activation of alkyl electrophiles in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions of amines, azoles and alkynes.

A detailed mechanistic study based on kinetic and density functional theory (DFT) calculations showed that oxidative addition occurs by two different routes, depending on the copper(I) species used: the ionic species undergoes an Sₙ2 reaction analogue and the neutral species reacts via a halogen-atom transfer (XAT) mechanism. Ligand acceleration of the neutral copper(I) species was observed, a phenomenon that is also present in the activation of alkyl electrophiles in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions of amines, azoles and alkynes.
